content.text: What Makes GenAI Bursts Different

Burst traffic in a GenAI system is harder to absorb than in a stateless API service because the mitigation options have wildly different latency profiles. Adding a web server replica takes 10–30 seconds. Loading a 70B LLM onto a new GPU pod takes 5–15 minutes — the model weights must be copied from object storage into GPU memory before the pod can serve a single token. This means the warm-up lag is a first-class architectural concern, not an incidental ops detail.

There are four distinct strategies for handling burst traffic, each with a different cost-latency trade-off. Effective capacity planning selects the right mix of strategies based on the burst's characteristics: whether it was predictable, how large it is, and how tight the user-facing SLA is.

attrs.alt: GenAI burst scaling strategies and decision flow

content.text: Strategy A: Warm GPU Pool

A warm pool pre-starts a fixed number of GPU pods with model weights already loaded. These pods sit idle (or serve low-priority keepalive requests) at baseline cost. When a burst arrives, they accept traffic within seconds — there is no weight-loading lag.
When to Use
Predictable daily peaks (9 AM office hours, end-of-day reporting batch)
Scheduled events with known lead time (product launches, marketing campaigns)
Interactive SLA ≤ 5 seconds where cold-start delay is unacceptable
Warm Pool Controller
Cost Model

Warm pool cost = (warm pod count) × (GPU instance hourly rate) × (hours). For an A100 instance at $3.50/h running 2 warm pods 24/7, the baseline cost is $168/month. Scale the pool only during predicted peak windows to reduce this. The warm pool cost must be weighed against the revenue impact of throttled interactive requests.
Strategy B: Provider Burst Quota

Most enterprise model provider accounts include a burst quota: a short-duration allowance to exceed the committed TPM limit, drawn from a reserve pool. OpenAI's Tier 4+ accounts, for example, allow brief bursts above the base TPM for spikes that last under a few minutes.
When to Use
Unexpected short spikes (< 5 minutes) that do not repeat frequently
Bridging the gap while Strategy A warm pods scale up
As a first-line absorber before routing to Strategy C
Burst Quota Tracker
Strategy C: Multi-Provider Fan-Out

When burst volume exceeds both the committed quota and the burst reserve, or when the burst is sustained, routing the overflow to a second model provider multiplies effective capacity. The routing layer must select the provider with available headroom and compatible model quality.
When to Use
Sustained bursts > 5 minutes that exhaust burst quota
High-availability requirements: if Provider A throttles, Provider B continues serving
Cost optimisation: route cost-insensitive batch work to a cheaper secondary provider
Overflow Router
Strategy D: Queue-Based Async Fulfillment

For requests that do not require an immediate synchronous response — document summarisation, batch report generation, offline analysis — the system can accept the request immediately with a 202 Accepted response, queue it for fulfillment within an SLA window, and deliver the result via polling or webhook. This strategy absorbs unlimited burst volume because the queue is the only scaling constraint, and queues scale far more cheaply than GPU pods.
When to Use
Batch workloads: report generation, document indexing, bulk translation
Non-urgent analysis: sentiment batch, content moderation queues
Any request where the user can tolerate a result in 1–10 minutes
As the final absorber when all synchronous paths are saturated
Async Job Queue

import asyncio
import uuid
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class JobStatus(Enum):
    QUEUED = "queued"
    PROCESSING = "processing"
    COMPLETED = "completed"
    FAILED = "failed"

@dataclass
class InferenceJob:
    job_id: str
    user_id: str
    messages: list[dict]
    priority: int = 2  # lower = higher priority
    status: JobStatus = JobStatus.QUEUED
    result: Optional[str] = None
    error: Optional[str] = None

class AsyncJobQueue:
    def __init__(self, sla_minutes: int = 10):
        self.sla_minutes = sla_minutes
        self._jobs: dict[str, InferenceJob] = {}
        self._queue: asyncio.PriorityQueue = asyncio.PriorityQueue()

    async def submit(
        self,
        user_id: str,
        messages: list[dict],
        priority: int = 2,
    ) -> str:
        job_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
        job = InferenceJob(job_id=job_id, user_id=user_id, messages=messages)
        self._jobs[job_id] = job
        await self._queue.put((priority, job_id))
        return job_id  # caller stores this and polls

    def get_status(self, job_id: str) -> Optional[InferenceJob]:
        return self._jobs.get(job_id)

    async def worker(self, inference_fn) -> None:
        while True:
            _, job_id = await self._queue.get()
            job = self._jobs[job_id]
            job.status = JobStatus.PROCESSING
            try:
                job.result = await inference_fn(job.messages)
                job.status = JobStatus.COMPLETED
            except Exception as exc:
                job.error = str(exc)
                job.status = JobStatus.FAILED
            finally:
                self._queue.task_done()

# HTTP handler pattern:
# POST /inference/async -> 202 {"job_id": "..."}
# GET  /inference/async/{job_id} -> {"status": "completed", "result": "..."}
Combining Strategies: The Burst Absorption Cascade
Production systems combine all four strategies in a cascade. Each layer absorbs what it can; overflow passes to the next layer:
Burst arrives → check warm pool availability (Strategy A). If warm slots available → serve immediately.
Warm pool saturated → draw from provider burst quota (Strategy B). If within burst window → serve immediately.
Burst quota exhausted → route to secondary provider (Strategy C). If secondary has headroom → serve immediately.
All synchronous paths saturated → classify by SLA. Interactive requests → 429 with Retry-After. Batch requests → queue for async fulfillment (Strategy D) → return 202.
Predictive Pre-Scaling

Reactive scaling always lags. For predictable bursts (product launches, scheduled batch runs, end-of-day peaks), pre-scale before the traffic arrives. A pre-scaling controller watches an event calendar and fires scale-up events N minutes before the expected burst:
Burst Scaling Metrics to Instrument
Metric
Description
Alert condition
burst_requests_total{strategy}
Counter of requests handled by each strategy
Distribution across cascade layers
warm_pool_utilization
Warm pods in use / total warm pods
Alert when > 85% — pre-scale trigger
queue_depth{priority}
Current depth of async job queue
Alert when > 80% of MAX_QUEUE_DEPTH
burst_quota_remaining_tpm
Provider burst headroom remaining
Alert when < 20% of burst window
overflow_provider_utilization
Secondary provider TPM used / limit
Alert when > 70% — may need third provider
p99_queue_wait_s
Time from async submission to start of processing
Alert when > SLA target
Summary

Burst scaling for GenAI systems requires four complementary strategies because no single mechanism handles all burst shapes. Warm GPU pools provide instant response to predictable peaks at the cost of always-on GPU spend. Provider burst quota absorbs brief unexpected spikes without infrastructure lag. Multi-provider fan-out multiplies capacity and adds resilience for sustained bursts. Queue-based async fulfillment absorbs unlimited burst volume for non-interactive workloads at minimal cost. In production, these four strategies cascade: each layer absorbs what it can, and overflow passes to the next. Predictive pre-scaling fires N minutes before known events to eliminate the reactive lag entirely. Instrument all four layers independently so the burst response can be tuned as traffic patterns evolve.
